It makes AI feel less like a blank chat box and more like a creative partner: one that can ask questions, generate options, expose sliders, accept annotations, and turn rough intent into something visual.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBut the closer you get to production, the more the workflow depends on handoff: to a coding agent, to the repo, and to the team's review process.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBuilder takes a different path. Instead of making a better artifact to hand off, it gives designers a visual way to work in the existing app, with the repo, preview, branch, and PR flow already handled.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eClaude Design is for exploring what should exist. to...\",\"builder-mok6usgp-48\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":740,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":739},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":741},\"\u003cp\u003eIf you still want to use Claude Design, but you’re interested in Builder, \u003ca href=\\\"https://www.builder.io/c/docs/integrate-with-claude-design\\\"\u003ewe also support importing your Claude Design projects\u003c/a\u003e at any phase of your workflow.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-2p\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":746,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":745},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":747},\"\u003ch3\u003eYou can still use Claude Design with Builder\u003c/h3\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-47\",[750,751,752,753],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":773,\"_186\":774,\"_128\":775},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":767,\"_186\":768,\"_128\":769},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":760,\"_186\":761,\"_128\":762},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":754,\"_186\":755,\"_128\":756},\"builder-mok6usgp-46\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":758,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":757},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":759},\"\u003cp\u003eAny changes you make use your real design tokens and real code components. And you can test them out against real data in your application.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhen you’re done, you can tag in the proper team for review, and they can continue work on the same branch, making sure that you’re prototyping effort—the tokens, time, and taste you put in—don’t go to waste. No more design fidelity loss.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThis also makes it far easier to make small design tweaks that would otherwise be backlogged for eternity. You might be browsing your team’s marketing site and notice a button that needs to move 2px to the left. Great. Just open the editor, move the button, and hit submit.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYour engineers always get final say, so you’ll never break production. You’re free to play around without worrying about jumping between a bunch of different tools. And if you do still want to \u003ca href=\\\"https://www.builder.io/blog/turn-a-figma-landing-page-into-a-live-website\\\"\u003eroundtrip with Figma\u003c/a\u003e, that’s completely supported.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-2k\",{\"_81\":198,\"_199\":764,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":763},{\"_167\":195},{\"_202\":765,\"_216\":766,\"_206\":14,\"_204\":205,\"_207\":205,\"_208\":205,\"_209\":205,\"_210\":211,\"_214\":205,\"_152\":215,\"_218\":205,\"_212\":213},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/file/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fb9db4e00f2d3442cb702b933edbed6d7\",0.5506024096385542,\"builder-mok6usgp-45\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":771,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":770},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":772},\"\u003cp\u003eWe’ve thought about designers, AI, and handoffs and awful lot, and we’ve designed common workflows right into our product.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eClaude Design is strong when you want to explore what should exist. \u003ca href=\\\"https://builder.io/blog/fusion\\\"\u003eBuilder\u003c/a\u003e is stronger when you need to safely change what already exists.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBuilder connects to your team’s repo one time (devs can set this up), and then anyone on your team can access \u003ca href=\\\"https://www.builder.io/blog/git-branching-for-designers\\\"\u003etheir own branches\u003c/a\u003e with a live preview, a visual editor, and AI (Claude, GPT, whatever you like) that can see and manipulate your real app inside a safe sandbox.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-2g\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":777,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":776},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":778},\"\u003ch2\u003eA better design handoff exists\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-44\",[781,782],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":789,\"_186\":790,\"_128\":791},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":783,\"_186\":784,\"_128\":785},\"builder-mok6usgp-43\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":787,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":786},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":788},\"\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eBecause the code Claude Design generates doesn’t use your team’s stack, design tokens, or real components, it’s just a prototype.\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAnd that’s fine. Anthropic has said that Claude Design is meant to export to Claude Code, which can read a codebase, work in a real repo, make changes across files, run tests, and deliver code.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBut realistically, \u003ca href=\\\"https://www.builder.io/blog/claude-code-for-designers\\\"\u003eClaude Code is hard for designers to use.\u003c/a\u003e It’s powerful, but it’s a developer-oriented tool. It asks you to deal with local repos, dependency installation, environment variables, app preview servers, Git branches, commits, pull requests, syncing, and merge conflicts.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAnd if all that doesn’t scare you, then it makes way more sense just to start there anyway. Claude Design is an extra step that, frankly, sits in a really awkward middle spot between Figma and Claude Code.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou might as well stick to Figma.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eOf course, most designers on a bigger team would want to use Claude Design and then hand off to engineers, much like you would from Figma.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThis is fine, in theory, but no one really saved any time. Your engineers now have to recreate your implementation from scratch, which may divert from the existing design system in subtle ways. You’re back to \u003ca href=\\\"https://www.builder.io/blog/designers-can-ship-without-engineering-handoffs\\\"\u003eall the existing problems with design handoffs.\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-28\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":793,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":792},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":794},\"\u003ch3\u003eHandoffs are ugly\u003c/h3\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-42\",[797,798,799,800],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":829,\"_186\":830,\"_128\":831},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":823,\"_186\":824,\"_128\":825},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":807,\"_186\":808,\"_128\":809},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":801,\"_186\":802,\"_128\":803},\"builder-mok6usgp-41\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":805,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":804},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":806},\"\u003cp\u003eAnd in my testing, its generations produced something that looked directionally related to the product I gave it as context.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBut there's a big difference between approximating a design system and working from the real one. The output doesn’t use our real code components. It has no 1:1 relationship with the existing implementation our team already worked hard on.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eThe fidelity was maybe 50%-75% at best: close enough to feel relevant at a glance, but not close enough to trust as a production representation.\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAnd unfortunately, it’s the same with importing your code repository. Claude uses the code as \u003cem\u003econtext\u003c/em\u003e, but it doesn’t actually work inside your repo or run your app to look at the rendered state. It’s just glancing through the files to get the vibe.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-23\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":812,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":810},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},\"Image\",{\"_22\":813,\"_216\":814,\"_815\":816,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F25a08efa12584e79a28a255239237fcf?width=780\",0.652,\"altText\",\"The Builder.io Design System interface in a web browser showing a file list of various font files and a selected CSS stylesheet named colors_and_type.css, with a sidebar for comments and a bottom section for uploading project assets.\",\"backgroundSize\",\"backgroundPosition\",\"lazy\",\"lockAspectRatio\",\"sizes\",\"(max-width: 638px) 86vw, (max-width: 998px) 66vw, 56vw\",\"builder-mok6usgp-40\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":827,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":826},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":828},\"\u003cp\u003eThe “start from scratch” wouldn’t be such a big problem if there were reliable ways to get your current application state into Claude Design in the first place.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eFor this, Claude Design has several options, in addition to screenshots and your own prompt engineering:\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\u003cli\u003eFigma import.\u003c/li\u003e\u003cli\u003eCustom design system.\u003c/li\u003e\u003cli\u003eCode import.\u003c/li\u003e\u003c/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWe already talked about some of the frustrations of the Figma import not being fine-grained enough and not having roundtrip back to Figma. But a bigger point is just… you’re adding another tool to a workflow that ideally wouldn’t need it.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBecause if Claude Design *knew *your codebase and design system perfectly, you could use its AI to start from scratch without messing around in Figma.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eSo, let’s look at the design system features, which a lot of designers are really hyped about.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThe setup flow is really straightforward, and Claude generates a basic design system references your brand’s typography, colors, and even spacing values. This part was easy to do.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-1v\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":833,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":832},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":834},\"\u003ch3\u003eContext isn't continuity\u003c/h3\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3z\",[837,838],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":845,\"_186\":846,\"_128\":847},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":839,\"_186\":840,\"_128\":841},\"builder-mok6usgp-3y\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":843,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":842},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":844},\"\u003cp\u003eClaude Design feels so lightweight because no part of it is your real app. Even though it can reference your code, it can’t run it. It doesn’t sync your latest production code. It doesn’t preserve any relationship with your app as it changes.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIf you’re doing a one-off concept, that’s not a problem. But it breaks over time.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eImagine you use Claude Design to explore a new marketing page, hand it off to your engineers, and then the site changes a bit over the next few months.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhen you next come back to that same part of the product, you can’t just open up your old Claude Design project, because it won’t be a real representation of your app. You have to start fresh: new project, re-add context, generate from scratch again.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThe problem here is that most product design isn’t net-new ideation. It’s returning to a surface that already exists, understanding what changed, making another iteration, and getting that change reviewed.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-1p\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":849,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":848},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":850},\"\u003ch3\u003eFast, but not persistent\u003c/h3\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3x\",[853,854],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":861,\"_186\":862,\"_128\":863},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":855,\"_186\":856,\"_128\":857},\"builder-mok6usgp-3w\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":859,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":858},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":860},\"\u003cp\u003eClaude Design is a fast tool for design exploration and a genuinely innovative way for designers to work with AI. Other than the actual AI generations, which take minutes, clicking around the interface lets you hop between ideas quickly and narrow down the sometimes overwhelming space of design.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBut it starts to fall apart is when you use it as part of a team working on an ongoing application.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eLet me explain what I mean.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-1l\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":865,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":864},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":866},\"\u003ch2\u003eClaude Design breaks for team workflows\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3v\",[869,870],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":877,\"_186\":878,\"_128\":879},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":871,\"_186\":872,\"_128\":873},\"builder-mok6usgp-3u\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":875,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":874},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":876},\"\u003cp\u003eThere’s several other rough edges in Claude Design, especially given it’s such a new product.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eFigma support is one of the biggest examples. In my testing, I couldn’t work with the Figma MCP or individual Figma frames. I had to upload and entire .fig file, which is really awkward when I just wanted to bring over one flow, frame, or component state and then had to create a whole new file instead.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eHistory and branching is another gap. There’s no way to go back in the AI chat history, which means that if the AI messes up your design, you’re stuck with it. Plus, I found that sometimes switching between commenting, annotating, editing, and other modes sometimes auto-sent things to the model before I was ready.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eOverall, I felt a big lack of fine-grained control. It’s a frustrating tool once you’re ready to refine the design. **And, crucially, there’s no export to Figma. **This means you’re stuck with whatever you make.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-1g\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":881,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":880},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":882},\"\u003ch2\u003eClaude Design feels early when you need production-level fidelity\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3t\",[885,886,887,888],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":908,\"_186\":909,\"_128\":910},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":902,\"_186\":903,\"_128\":904},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":895,\"_186\":896,\"_128\":897},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":889,\"_186\":890,\"_128\":891},\"builder-mok6usgp-3s\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":893,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":892},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":894},\"\u003cp\u003eThat said, the feature is currently buggy. When you get multiple design options generated, the canvas mode changes to a more traditional “pan around the page” design mode, and you can’t scroll or easily interact with the static-ish mocks it presents. (The tweak option in the bottom right of my screenshot was not working.)\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eHopefully, this gets fixed soon, because as it is, the feature isn’t super usable.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-1d\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":899,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":898},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":900,\"_216\":814,\"_815\":901,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F20a9a1cdbaf248198215aa637b4c384e?width=780\",\"Claude AI interface showing a web design workspace with a \\\"tweaks\\\" panel in the bottom right corner offering toggles for \\\"all four\\\" or \\\"one full-bleed\\\" design layouts.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3r\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":906,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":905},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":907},\"\u003cp\u003eThe last really cool thing I appreciated about Claude Design is that it can generate multiple options per prompt turn and then lay them out on the same canvas for you to compare.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eSure, it takes the AI much longer to do (and uses way more tokens), but often, designing is about thinking outside the box, and I found it super helpful to tell the model to generate 4 vastly different-looking things for me to bounce ideas around.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-1a\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":912,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":911},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":913},\"\u003ch2\u003eMultiple design options are great, but the canvas gets in the way\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3q\",[916,917,918,919],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":939,\"_186\":940,\"_128\":941},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":933,\"_186\":934,\"_128\":935},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":926,\"_186\":927,\"_128\":928},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":920,\"_186\":921,\"_128\":922},\"builder-mok6usgp-3p\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":924,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":923},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":925},\"\u003cp\u003ePlus, it limits what you can do by the element selected. If you select a simple sentence, you get font-related options. If you select a grid, you get layout options. Just because all options are available in code all the time doesn’t mean Claude Design shows you a massive panel of unrelated controls, like other tools. It shows the ones that you actually need.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAll that said, what you can actually edit on the page is pretty limited. \u003cstrong\u003eYou can’t just grab elements and freely move them around like you would in Figma or Builder.\u003c/strong\u003e For anything bigger than borders, colors, font options, and margins, you’re back to talking to the AI.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThere’s a tension here. Claude Design has this great visual surface, but it’s not a flexible design canvas in the way you’d expect. You get *some *direct control, but not enough to get real design work done without resorting to a game of AI telephone.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-16\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":930,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":929},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":931,\"_216\":814,\"_815\":932,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F403b065d590f45058891d477f788ab4e?width=780\",\"Interface showing a Claude Design project with a blog-style layout, side-panel property editors, and a post titled How to Build Agent-Native Applications.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3o\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":937,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":936},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":938},\"\u003cp\u003eNow, for the big one. As designers, we want to get our hands dirty and move things around ourselves on the page.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eHere, I have to say, I’m both pleased and pretty disappointed.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eOn the one hand, Claude Design’s edit mode feels way more designer-oriented than most AI coding tools, using language like “tracking” instead of arbitrary CSS values that only devs know.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-12\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":943,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":942},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":944},\"\u003ch2\u003eEdit mode doesn't quite give designers enough control\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3n\",[947,948,949,950,951,952],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":985,\"_186\":986,\"_128\":987},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":979,\"_186\":980,\"_128\":981},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":972,\"_186\":973,\"_128\":974},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":966,\"_186\":967,\"_128\":968},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":959,\"_186\":960,\"_128\":961},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":953,\"_186\":954,\"_128\":955},\"builder-mok6usgp-3m\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":957,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":956},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":958},\"\u003cp\u003eIn these ways, Claude Design feels genuinely designer-oriented. Instead of focusing on ways to type, it gives you ways to point.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-10\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":963,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":962},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":964,\"_216\":814,\"_815\":965,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F769db8673f9549d890662e459943a697?width=780\",\"A user’s browser window showing a design collaboration tool where a rectangular comment box is attached to a website header, displaying the text \\\"comments stick to elements\\\" alongside a \\\"Comment\\\" button and a \\\"Send to Claude\\\" button.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3l\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":970,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":969},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":971},\"\u003cp\u003eSometimes you don't want to describe a layout in paragraphs. You want to draw an arrow, circle an area, sketch a rough shape, and say, \\\"more like this.\\\" For that, Design also lets you annotate and comment directly on the project.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-y\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":976,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":975},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":977,\"_216\":814,\"_815\":978,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F27e639861d5242ef97befa7ac9de1b8f?width=780\",\"Claude Design interface featuring a chat panel on the left and a whiteboard-style canvas on the right, with a hand-drawn sticky note being added to the workspace.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3k\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":983,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":982},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":984},\"\u003cp\u003eAnother thing AI design tools should let designers do is communicate visually with the model.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eClaude Design’s scratchpad feature is a nice example here. It's a lightweight drawing surface available to you even when the agent is working (thanks to Claude Design’s tabbed workflow), then send your doodles to the agent as context.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-v\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":989,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":988},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":990},\"\u003ch2\u003eVisual feedback makes Claude Design feel designer-native\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3j\",[993,994,995,996,997,998],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1033,\"_186\":1034,\"_128\":1035},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1027,\"_186\":1028,\"_128\":1029},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1019,\"_186\":1020,\"_128\":1021},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1013,\"_186\":1014,\"_128\":1015},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1005,\"_186\":1006,\"_128\":1007},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":999,\"_186\":1000,\"_128\":1001},\"builder-mok6usgp-3i\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1003,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1002},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1004},\"\u003cp\u003eDesign, and especially design with AI, is about taste and restraint. Usually AI models go overboard (or don’t do enough) of whatever it is you tell them to do. So, being able to play with a slider that tweaks spacing or intensity in realtime if super helpful.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThat said, the feature as it is today is still pretty buggy. Nobody has figured out how to do great generative UI yet, and Anthropic is no exception. Sometimes tweaks are mutually exclusive, and not all permutations actually react to your intent. And I often wanted more tuning parameters and less drastically different options.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBut the product idea is strong: AI design tools should give designers tweakable ranges instead of hard answers.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-r\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":1009,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1008},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":1010,\"_216\":1011,\"_815\":1012,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fdcc4310599344a18bd616ce0ba808161?width=780\",0.562429696287964,\"Fred Armisen as a Venezuelan official in a Parks and Recreation scene, looking serious and holding a microphone, with a text overlay that reads \\\"BELIEVE IT OR NOT, PROMPT THE MODEL.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3h\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1017,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1016},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1018},\"\u003cp\u003eI found this to be a genuinely cool interaction model. Most AI design tools make every refinement conversational. Want more spacing? Prompt the model. Want a darker background? Prompt the model. Want the hero to feel more editorial and less SaaS-y?\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-p\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":1023,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1022},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":1024,\"_216\":1025,\"_815\":1026,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?width=780\",0.6465,\"Claude Design interface displaying a prompt field and a series of clarifying questions in the canvas area. Claude Design interface showcasing the Tweaks panel with sliders and toggle controls for adjusting design parameters. Claude Design interface showing a UI hero section with a sidebar highlighting spacing and intensity adjustment sliders. A design workspace featuring a scratchpad area with handwritten annotations, arrows, and circles overlaid on the UI. Claude Design screen showing a UI component with comment pins and annotation bubbles indicating visual feedback. A CSS-style property panel in Claude Design showing specific controls like tracking, text size, and layout settings for a selected element. A comparison view in Claude Design showing four distinct layout variations of a landing page displayed side-by-side on the canvas. A setup interface for a design system in Claude Design showing configured brand typography, color palettes, and spacing values.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3g\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1031,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1030},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1032},\"\u003cp\u003eAnother really interesting idea in Claude Design is the Tweaks panel.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhen Claude generates a design, it also generates custom controls for that design: sliders, toggles, and adjustable parameters that let you change parts of the result without asking the model to regenerate everything. (If you’ve ever worked on video games, it’s a lot like building your own custom tooling to make assets.)\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-m\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1037,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1036},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1038},\"\u003ch2\u003eTweaks instead of re-prompting\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3f\",[1041,1042,1043,1044],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1064,\"_186\":1065,\"_128\":1066},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1058,\"_186\":1059,\"_128\":1060},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1051,\"_186\":1052,\"_128\":1053},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1045,\"_186\":1046,\"_128\":1047},\"builder-mok6usgp-3e\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1049,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1048},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1050},\"\u003cp\u003eIt’s a little thing, but I find the expanded interface, and the knowledge that there will always be lots of clarifications, where I have space to doodle my thoughts or select multiple answers to the same questions, to be very freeing.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAnd I found that the questions were very relevant to what we were designing, too.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eThis fits my design workflow. I don’t always know what I want when I’m starting at a text-heavy chat thread and a blank canvas. Instead of trying to describe the perfect result and know about all my context beforehand, I get to lean on the AI to do what it does best: go figure out the mundane things for me and just ask me about questions of taste.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAnd sometimes you just need the tool to show you a few directions, ask what feels right, and help you narrow the space. It’s less “type prompt, receive UI” and more like a guided creative process.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-h\",{\"_81\":811,\"_199\":1055,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1054},{\"_167\":195,\"_152\":153,\"_161\":162},{\"_22\":1056,\"_216\":814,\"_815\":1057,\"_817\":211,\"_818\":215,\"_819\":14,\"_214\":205,\"_820\":14,\"_821\":822},\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F1647bbd9ee4c48c1abac546f655cedeb?width=780\",\"Claude Design interface showing a browser window with a blog layout redesign project, featuring a workspace with configuration questions, layout options, and project settings.\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3d\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1062,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1061},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1063},\"\u003cp\u003eMy favorite thing about Claude Design is that it doesn't make every interaction feel like prompt engineering.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIn my testing, I told it things as simple as “make me a new homepage” or “redesign the blog hero” and that was enough for it to get going.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBecause after your simple prompt, it asks you questions. A lot of questions. This is more than just a quick question tool in the prompt window. Claude Design uses the entire canvas to give you a taste exam.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-d\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1068,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1067},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1069},\"\u003ch2\u003ePrompting with designers in mind\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3c\",[1072,1073],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1080,\"_186\":1081,\"_128\":1082},{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1074,\"_186\":1075,\"_128\":1076},\"builder-mok6usgp-3b\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1078,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1077},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1079},\"\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\\\"https://claude.ai/design\\\"\u003eClaude Design\u003c/a\u003e is a browser-based tool from Anthropic Labs for generating and iterating on visual work with Claude. They position it for prototypes, product concepts, decks, one-pagers, and marketing assets rather than app generation alone.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can start from a prompt, upload assets, bring in documents, capture an existing website, or feed it codebase context. Claude then generates a visual project you can refine through chat, annotations, comments, direct edits, and even generated controls. Then, you export it or hand it off to your actual coding agent.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eClaude Design is completely different from Claude Code.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\u003cli\u003eClaude Code is the agentic coding system that reads your local codebases, makes cross-file changes, runs tests, and delivers committed code.\u003c/li\u003e\u003cli\u003eClaude Design is a separate visual surface that produces a handoff bundle when you're ready to implement. (Design is actually a separate app that’s not bundled the Claude desktop app, which I found a bit disappointing for workflow.)\u003c/li\u003e\u003c/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003ePut simply, Claude Code is focused on devs, and Claude Design is focused on designers.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgo-7\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1084,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1083},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1085},\"\u003ch2\u003eWhat Claude Design is\u003c/h2\u003e\",\"builder-mok6usgp-3a\",[1088],{\"_114\":115,\"_116\":117,\"_72\":1089,\"_186\":1090,\"_128\":1091},\"builder-mok6usgp-39\",{\"_81\":286,\"_199\":1093,\"_201\":-5},{\"_130\":1092},{\"_167\":195},{\"_271\":1094},\"\u003cp\u003eSo, is Figma dead yet?\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eI spent a few days with Anthropic’s new tool, Claude Design, to figure out that answer.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAnd the thing is, Claude Design isn’t really trying to be Figma. Or Lovable, for that matter. It's more just… a design workspace, where iteration feels more like whiteboarding than prompt engineering.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eI’m pleasantly surprised. The good stuff is genuinely good: a canvas that asks you questions, generates multiple design options, gives you sliders to tweak instead of re-prompting, lets you annotate directly, and bundles everything up for your coding agent of choice when you're ready to build.\u003c/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eBut it’s also an early product, with a lot of cruft. Claude Design is best at helping you figure out what a product \u003cem\u003ecould\u003c/em\u003e look like. It's much less useful once your designs need to live inside real, changing applications. (But no worries, I'll cover how to work through that at the end.)\u003c/p\u003e\",\"claude\",\"claude design\",\"design to code\",\"@builder.io/core:Reference\",\"724dd11feeb549f0b6fc12b6e4741c19\",\"model\",\"blog-author\",{\"_67\":1103,\"_72\":1099,\"_81\":64,\"_79\":1104,\"_83\":83,\"_84\":1105,\"_20\":1106,\"_88\":1107,\"_74\":1108,\"_94\":1109,\"_86\":561,\"_65\":1110,\"_76\":555,\"_70\":1111,\"_77\":1112,\"_99\":1113},1742917398633,\"2bef325751454d03b0dfb22ce8a7be76\",[],{\"_1115\":1116,\"_42\":64,\"_1117\":205,\"_1118\":1119,\"_1120\":1121,\"_26\":1122,\"_24\":1123},{},1773848125803,1742917469709,\"5d6wdRTSjOOGM7rbQspFnuSFdNS2\",[],{\"_318\":20,\"_321\":322,\"_314\":1114,\"_319\":275,\"_316\":14},\"7oik7zdurxh\",{\"_134\":324,\"_132\":323},\"bio\",\"\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eAlice Moore\u003c/strong\u003e\u0026nbsp;loved writing about obscure JavaScript frameworks before AI decided everything should be React. Now, she mostly writes about the intricacies of AI. As a DevRel engineer at\u0026nbsp;Builder.io, she’s always looking for ways to make agentic products—and the web—more usable for everyone.\u0026nbsp;Outside work, you can find her consuming a frankly unhealthy amount of fantasy and sci-fi.\u003c/p\u003e\",\"showOnAuthorsPage\",\"photo\",\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F8f7c8163d6e34ce39671fe76478a52e8\",\"socialMedia\",[1124,1125,1126],\"DevRel\",\"alice-moore\",{\"_1127\":1136,\"_1129\":1137,\"_1131\":1138},{\"_1129\":1133,\"_1131\":1134,\"_1127\":1135},{\"_1127\":1128,\"_1129\":1130,\"_1131\":1132},\"platformName\",\"Personal Site\",\"profileLink\",\"https://alicealexandra.com/\",\"platformIcon\",\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F6831794b2b30439d99ef8b0aed42ba80\",\"https://github.com/3mdistal/\",\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F297bdcf0f4134370906a4ffe7f8d2bd6\",\"GitHub\",\"LinkedIn\",\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/alice-alexandra-moore/\",\"https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F0191ca45379f42dabbed7b8a2d17ea4e\"]\n");</script><script>window.__reactRouterContext.streamController.close();</script><script>

.builder-brand-tokens {
  /* ── Fonts ── */
  --b-font-sans: 'Geist', system-ui, sans-serif;
  --b-font-mono: 'Geist Mono', monospace;

  /* ── Color primitives ── */
  --c-violet-400: #A8C1FF;
  --c-blue-100: #BEF0F5;
  --c-blue-200: #7BE8F5;
  --c-blue-300: #00DFF6;
  --c-blue-400: #00D6F6;
  --c-blue-500: #01C8F1;
  --c-blue-500-40p: #01C8F166;
  --c-blue-600: #009CCC;
  --c-blue-900: #00161B;
  --c-green-400: #0FDFBA;
  --c-yellow-400: #DEC75F;
  --c-red-400: #FFA27D;
  --c-pink-400: #FA9BF2;
  --c-neutral-50: #FAF9F5;
  --c-neutral-100: #E0E0D7;
  --c-neutral-200: #CAC9C6;
  --c-neutral-400: #ABABAB;
  --c-neutral-500: #9A9997;
  --c-neutral-600: #5E5E5E;
  --c-neutral-700: #3D3D3D;
  --c-neutral-800: #2E2E2E;
  --c-neutral-900: #1A1A1A;
  --c-neutral-925: #0F0F0F;
  --c-neutral-950: #0A0A0A;
  --c-neutral-950-90p: #0A0A0AE6;
  --c-white-5p: #FFFFFF0D;

  /* ── Semantic: bg ── */
  --b-bg-page: #0A0A0A;
  --b-bg-surface: #0A0A0A;
  --b-bg-raised: #0F0F0F;
  --b-bg-prominent: #1A1A1A;
  --b-bg-translucent: #0A0A0AE6;
  --b-bg-alternative: #00161B;

  /* ── Semantic: text ── */
  --b-text-primary: #FAF9F5;
  --b-text-secondary: #9A9997;
  --b-text-muted: #5E5E5E;
  --b-text-highlight: #E0E0D7;
  --b-text-link: #BEF0F5;
  --b-text-eyebrow: #01C8F1;

  /* ── Semantic: border ── */
  --b-border-default: #2E2E2E;
  --b-border-subtle: #FFFFFF0D;
  --b-border-menu: #1a1a1a;

  /* ── Semantic: action ── */
  --b-action-primary-bg: #01C8F1;
  --b-action-primary-hover: #00DFF6;
  --b-action-primary-text: #0A0A0A;
  --b-action-primary-border: #00DFF6;
  --b-action-primary-effect: #01C8F166;
  --b-action-secondary-bg: transparent;
  --b-action-secondary-hover: #1A1A1A;
  --b-action-secondary-text: #FAF9F5;
  --b-action-secondary-border: #5E5E5E;
  --b-action-tab-bg: #0A0A0A;
  --b-action-tab-bg-active: #00161B;
  --b-action-tab-bg-hover: #0F0F0F;
  --b-action-tab-text: #5E5E5E;
  --b-action-tab-text-active: #BEF0F5;
  --b-action-tab-text-hover: #FAF9F5;
  --b-action-tab-indicator: #01C8F1;

  /* ── Utility ── */
  --b-btn-hover-bg: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.08);

  /* ── Border radius ── */
  --b-radius: 6px;
  --b-radius-none: 0px;
  --b-radius-sm: 3px;
  --b-radius-md: 6px;
  --b-radius-lg: 12px;
  --b-radius-full: 9999px;

  /* ── Gradient ── */
  --b-gradient-blue: linear-gradient(90deg, #18B4F4 0%, #19B9F5 6.25%, #1BBDF5 12.5%, #1CC2F6 18.75%, #1EC7F7 25%, #1FCBF7 31.25%, #21D0F8 37.5%, #22D5F9 43.75%, #24D9F9 50%, #25DEFA 56.25%, #26E3FB 62.5%, #28E8FC 68.75%, #29ECFC 75%, #2BF1FD 81.25%, #2CF6FE 87.5%, #2EFAFE 93.75%, #2FFFFF 100%);

  /* ── Spacing scale (token key × 4px) ── */
  --spacing-0: 0px;
  --spacing-1: 4px;
  --spacing-2: 8px;
  --spacing-3: 12px;
  --spacing-4: 16px;
  --spacing-5: 20px;
  --spacing-6: 24px;
  --spacing-8: 32px;
  --spacing-10: 40px;
  --spacing-12: 48px;
  --spacing-16: 64px;
  --spacing-20: 80px;
  --spacing-24: 96px;
  --spacing-30: 120px;
  --spacing-35: 150px;
  --spacing-50: 200px;

  /* ── Typography scale ── */
  --b-t-heading-1: 56px;
  --b-t-heading-2: 46px;
  --b-t-heading-3: 37px;
  --b-t-heading-4: 28px;
  --b-t-heading-5: 23px;
  --b-t-heading-6: 18px;
  --b-t-paragraph-1: 18px;
  --b-t-paragraph-2: 15px;
  --b-t-paragraph-2-md: 15px;
  --b-t-paragraph-3: 12px;
  --b-t-label-1: 14px;
  --b-t-label-2: 11px;
}

@media (max-width: 768px) {
  .builder-brand-tokens {
    --spacing-4: 12px;
    --spacing-5: 16px;
    --spacing-6: 20px;
    --spacing-8: 24px;
    --spacing-10: 24px;
    --spacing-12: 24px;
    --spacing-16: 24px;
    --spacing-20: 32px;
    --spacing-24: 32px;
    --spacing-30: 72px;
    --spacing-35: 88px;
    --spacing-50: 80px;

    --b-t-heading-1: 32px;
    --b-t-heading-2: 28px;

    --b-t-heading-4: 22px;
    --b-t-heading-5: 19px;
    --b-t-heading-6: 16px;
    --b-t-paragraph-1: 16px;
    --b-t-paragraph-2: 15px;
    --b-t-paragraph-3: 12px;
    --b-t-label-1: 14px;
    --b-t-label-2: 11px;
  }
}

.builder-brand-tokens .blue-gradient {
  background: var(--b-gradient-blue);
}

.builder-brand-tokens .blue-gradient-text {
  background: var(--b-gradient-blue);
  -webkit-background-clip: text;
  -webkit-text-fill-color: transparent;
  background-clip: text;
}

class="css-e5mewg"><div class="blog-article-container css-180xeld"><main><div class="builder-component builder-component-cba159374ab24ee3ad803b7e4daf1be3" data-name="blog-article" data-source="Rendered by Builder.io"><div class="builder-content" builder-content-id="cba159374ab24ee3ad803b7e4daf1be3" builder-model="blog-article"><div data-builder-component="blog-article" data-builder-content-id="cba159374ab24ee3ad803b7e4daf1be3"><div class="builder-blocks css-h47494" builder-type="blocks"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3a css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3a"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-39 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-39"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>So, is Figma dead yet?</p>
<p>I spent a few days with Anthropic’s new tool, Claude Design, to figure out that answer.</p>
<p>And the thing is, Claude Design isn’t really trying to be Figma. Or Lovable, for that matter. It's more just… a design workspace, where iteration feels more like whiteboarding than prompt engineering.</p>
<p>I’m pleasantly surprised. The good stuff is genuinely good: a canvas that asks you questions, generates multiple design options, gives you sliders to tweak instead of re-prompting, lets you annotate directly, and bundles everything up for your coding agent of choice when you're ready to build.</p>
<p>But it’s also an early product, with a lot of cruft. Claude Design is best at helping you figure out what a product <em>could</em> look like. It's much less useful once your designs need to live inside real, changing applications. (But no worries, I'll cover how to work through that at the end.)</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3c css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3c"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-7 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-7"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="what-claude-design-is" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "What Claude Design is" section" title="Click to copy link to "What Claude Design is" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">What Claude Design is<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3b builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3b"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p><a href="https://claude.ai/design">Claude Design</a> is a browser-based tool from Anthropic Labs for generating and iterating on visual work with Claude. They position it for prototypes, product concepts, decks, one-pagers, and marketing assets rather than app generation alone.</p>
<p>You can start from a prompt, upload assets, bring in documents, capture an existing website, or feed it codebase context. Claude then generates a visual project you can refine through chat, annotations, comments, direct edits, and even generated controls. Then, you export it or hand it off to your actual coding agent.</p>
<p>Claude Design is completely different from Claude Code.</p>
<ul><li>Claude Code is the agentic coding system that reads your local codebases, makes cross-file changes, runs tests, and delivers committed code.</li><li>Claude Design is a separate visual surface that produces a handoff bundle when you're ready to implement. (Design is actually a separate app that’s not bundled the Claude desktop app, which I found a bit disappointing for workflow.)</li></ul>
<p>Put simply, Claude Code is focused on devs, and Claude Design is focused on designers.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3f css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3f"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-d builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-d"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="prompting-with-designers-in-mind" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Prompting with designers in mind" section" title="Click to copy link to "Prompting with designers in mind" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Prompting with designers in mind<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3d builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3d"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>My favorite thing about Claude Design is that it doesn't make every interaction feel like prompt engineering.</p>
<p>In my testing, I told it things as simple as “make me a new homepage” or “redesign the blog hero” and that was enough for it to get going.</p>
<p>Because after your simple prompt, it asks you questions. A lot of questions. This is more than just a quick question tool in the prompt window. Claude Design uses the entire canvas to give you a taste exam.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-h css-yo5ljq" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-h"><picture><source srcset="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F1647bbd9ee4c48c1abac546f655cedeb?format=webp&width=100 100w, class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3e builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3e"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>It’s a little thing, but I find the expanded interface, and the knowledge that there will always be lots of clarifications, where I have space to doodle my thoughts or select multiple answers to the same questions, to be very freeing.</p>
<p>And I found that the questions were very relevant to what we were designing, too.</p>
<p>This fits my design workflow. I don’t always know what I want when I’m starting at a text-heavy chat thread and a blank canvas. Instead of trying to describe the perfect result and know about all my context beforehand, I get to lean on the AI to do what it does best: go figure out the mundane things for me and just ask me about questions of taste.</p>
<p>And sometimes you just need the tool to show you a few directions, ask what feels right, and help you narrow the space. It’s less “type prompt, receive UI” and more like a guided creative process.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3j css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3j"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-m builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-m"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="tweaks-instead-of-re-prompting" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Tweaks instead of re-prompting" section" title="Click to copy link to "Tweaks instead of re-prompting" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Tweaks instead of re-prompting<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3g builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3g"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Another really interesting idea in Claude Design is the Tweaks panel.</p>
<p>When Claude generates a design, it also generates custom controls for that design: sliders, toggles, and adjustable parameters that let you change parts of the result without asking the model to regenerate everything. (If you’ve ever worked on video games, it’s a lot like building your own custom tooling to make assets.)</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-p css-yo5ljq" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-p"><picture><source srcset="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=100 100w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=200 200w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=400 400w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=800 800w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=1200 1200w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=1600 1600w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=2000 2000w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2Fd1791dc4c4a84711a10e90c1c7d95bfc?format=webp&width=780 780w" type="image/webp"><img alt="Claude Design interface displaying a prompt field and a series of clarifying questions in the canvas area. Most AI design tools make every refinement conversational. Want more spacing? Prompt the model. Want a darker background? Prompt the model. Want the hero to feel more editorial and less SaaS-y?</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-r css-yo5ljq" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-r"><picture><source class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3i builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3i"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Design, and especially design with AI, is about taste and restraint. Usually AI models go overboard (or don’t do enough) of whatever it is you tell them to do. So, being able to play with a slider that tweaks spacing or intensity in realtime if super helpful.</p>
<p>That said, the feature as it is today is still pretty buggy. Nobody has figured out how to do great generative UI yet, and Anthropic is no exception. Sometimes tweaks are mutually exclusive, and not all permutations actually react to your intent. And I often wanted more tuning parameters and less drastically different options.</p>
<p>But the product idea is strong: AI design tools should give designers tweakable ranges instead of hard answers.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3n css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3n"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-v builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-v"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="visual-feedback-makes-claude-design-feel-designer-native" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Visual feedback makes Claude Design feel designer-native" section" title="Click to copy link to "Visual feedback makes Claude Design feel designer-native" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Visual feedback makes Claude Design feel designer-native<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3k builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3k"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Another thing AI design tools should let designers do is communicate visually with the model.</p>
<p>Claude Design’s scratchpad feature is a nice example here. It's a lightweight drawing surface available to you even when the agent is working (thanks to Claude Design’s tabbed workflow), then send your doodles to the agent as context.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3m builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3m"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>In these ways, Claude Design feels genuinely designer-oriented. Instead of focusing on ways to type, it gives you ways to point.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3q css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3q"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-12 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-12"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="edit-mode-doesnt-quite-give-designers-enough-control" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Edit mode doesn't quite give designers enough control" section" title="Click to copy link to "Edit mode doesn't quite give designers enough control" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Edit mode doesn't quite give designers enough control<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3o builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3o"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Now, for the big one. As designers, we want to get our hands dirty and move things around ourselves on the page.</p>
<p>Here, I have to say, I’m both pleased and pretty disappointed.</p>
<p>On the one hand, Claude Design’s edit mode feels way more designer-oriented than most AI coding tools, using language like “tracking” instead of arbitrary CSS values that only devs know.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-16 css-yo5ljq" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-16"><picture><source srcset="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F403b065d590f45058891d477f788ab4e?format=webp&width=100 100w, src="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F403b065d590f45058891d477f788ab4e?width=780"></picture><div class="builder-image-sizer css-1jpkznw"> </div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3p builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3p"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Plus, it limits what you can do by the element selected. If you select a simple sentence, you get font-related options. If you select a grid, you get layout options. Just because all options are available in code all the time doesn’t mean Claude Design shows you a massive panel of unrelated controls, like other tools. It shows the ones that you actually need.</p>
<p>All that said, what you can actually edit on the page is pretty limited. <strong>You can’t just grab elements and freely move them around like you would in Figma or Builder.</strong> For anything bigger than borders, colors, font options, and margins, you’re back to talking to the AI.</p>
<p>There’s a tension here. Claude Design has this great visual surface, but it’s not a flexible design canvas in the way you’d expect. You get *some *direct control, but not enough to get real design work done without resorting to a game of AI telephone.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3t css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3t"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-1a builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-1a"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="multiple-design-options-are-great-but-the-canvas-gets-in-the-way" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Multiple design options are great, but the canvas gets in the way" section" title="Click to copy link to "Multiple design options are great, but the canvas gets in the way" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Multiple design options are great, but the canvas gets in the way<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3r builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3r"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>The last really cool thing I appreciated about Claude Design is that it can generate multiple options per prompt turn and then lay them out on the same canvas for you to compare.</p>
<p>Sure, it takes the AI much longer to do (and uses way more tokens), but often, designing is about thinking outside the box, and I found it super helpful to tell the model to generate 4 vastly different-looking things for me to bounce ideas around.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-1d css-yo5ljq" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-1d"><picture><source srcset="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F20a9a1cdbaf248198215aa637b4c384e?format=webp&width=100 100w, class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3s builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3s"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>That said, the feature is currently buggy. When you get multiple design options generated, the canvas mode changes to a more traditional “pan around the page” design mode, and you can’t scroll or easily interact with the static-ish mocks it presents. (The tweak option in the bottom right of my screenshot was not working.)</p>
<p>Hopefully, this gets fixed soon, because as it is, the feature isn’t super usable.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3v css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3v"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgo-1g builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgo-1g"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="claude-design-feels-early-when-you-need-production-level-fidelity" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Claude Design feels early when you need production-level fidelity" section" title="Click to copy link to "Claude Design feels early when you need production-level fidelity" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Claude Design feels early when you need production-level fidelity<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3u builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3u"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>There’s several other rough edges in Claude Design, especially given it’s such a new product.</p>
<p>Figma support is one of the biggest examples. In my testing, I couldn’t work with the Figma MCP or individual Figma frames. I had to upload and entire .fig file, which is really awkward when I just wanted to bring over one flow, frame, or component state and then had to create a whole new file instead.</p>
<p>History and branching is another gap. There’s no way to go back in the AI chat history, which means that if the AI messes up your design, you’re stuck with it. Plus, I found that sometimes switching between commenting, annotating, editing, and other modes sometimes auto-sent things to the model before I was ready.</p>
<p>Overall, I felt a big lack of fine-grained control. It’s a frustrating tool once you’re ready to refine the design. **And, crucially, there’s no export to Figma. **This means you’re stuck with whatever you make.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3x css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3x"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-1l builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-1l"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="claude-design-breaks-for-team-workflows" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "Claude Design breaks for team workflows" section" title="Click to copy link to "Claude Design breaks for team workflows" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Claude Design breaks for team workflows<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3w builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3w"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Claude Design is a fast tool for design exploration and a genuinely innovative way for designers to work with AI. Other than the actual AI generations, which take minutes, clicking around the interface lets you hop between ideas quickly and narrow down the sometimes overwhelming space of design.</p>
<p>But it starts to fall apart is when you use it as part of a team working on an ongoing application.</p>
<p>Let me explain what I mean.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3z css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3z"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-1p builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-1p"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h3>Fast, but not persistent</h3></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-3y builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-3y"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Claude Design feels so lightweight because no part of it is your real app. Even though it can reference your code, it can’t run it. It doesn’t sync your latest production code. It doesn’t preserve any relationship with your app as it changes.</p>
<p>If you’re doing a one-off concept, that’s not a problem. But it breaks over time.</p>
<p>Imagine you use Claude Design to explore a new marketing page, hand it off to your engineers, and then the site changes a bit over the next few months.</p>
<p>When you next come back to that same part of the product, you can’t just open up your old Claude Design project, because it won’t be a real representation of your app. You have to start fresh: new project, re-add context, generate from scratch again.</p>
<p>The problem here is that most product design isn’t net-new ideation. It’s returning to a surface that already exists, understanding what changed, making another iteration, and getting that change reviewed.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-42 css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-42"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-1v builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-1v"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h3>Context isn't continuity</h3></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-40 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-40"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>The “start from scratch” wouldn’t be such a big problem if there were reliable ways to get your current application state into Claude Design in the first place.</p>
<p>For this, Claude Design has several options, in addition to screenshots and your own prompt engineering:</p>
<ul><li>Figma import.</li><li>Custom design system.</li><li>Code import.</li></ul>
<p>We already talked about some of the frustrations of the Figma import not being fine-grained enough and not having roundtrip back to Figma. But a bigger point is just… you’re adding another tool to a workflow that ideally wouldn’t need it.</p>
<p>Because if Claude Design *knew *your codebase and design system perfectly, you could use its AI to start from scratch without messing around in Figma.</p>
<p>So, let’s look at the design system features, which a lot of designers are really hyped about.</p>
<p>The setup flow is really straightforward, and Claude generates a basic design system references your brand’s typography, colors, and even spacing values. This part was easy to do.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-23 css-yo5ljq" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-23"><picture><source srcset="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F25a08efa12584e79a28a255239237fcf?format=webp&width=100 100w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F25a08efa12584e79a28a255239237fcf?format=webp&width=200 200w, https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F25a08efa12584e79a28a255239237fcf?format=webp&width=400 400w, src="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/image/assets%2FYJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt%2F25a08efa12584e79a28a255239237fcf?width=780"></picture><div class="builder-image-sizer css-1jpkznw"> </div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-41 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-41"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>And in my testing, its generations produced something that looked directionally related to the product I gave it as context.</p>
<p>But there's a big difference between approximating a design system and working from the real one. The output doesn’t use our real code components. It has no 1:1 relationship with the existing implementation our team already worked hard on.</p>
<p><strong>The fidelity was maybe 50%-75% at best: close enough to feel relevant at a glance, but not close enough to trust as a production representation.</strong></p>
<p>And unfortunately, it’s the same with importing your code repository. Claude uses the code as <em>context</em>, but it doesn’t actually work inside your repo or run your app to look at the rendered state. It’s just glancing through the files to get the vibe.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-44 css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-44"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-28 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-28"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h3>Handoffs are ugly</h3></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-43 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-43"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p><strong>Because the code Claude Design generates doesn’t use your team’s stack, design tokens, or real components, it’s just a prototype.</strong></p>
<p>And that’s fine. Anthropic has said that Claude Design is meant to export to Claude Code, which can read a codebase, work in a real repo, make changes across files, run tests, and deliver code.</p>
<p>But realistically, <a href="https://www.builder.io/blog/claude-code-for-designers">Claude Code is hard for designers to use.</a> It’s powerful, but it’s a developer-oriented tool. It asks you to deal with local repos, dependency installation, environment variables, app preview servers, Git branches, commits, pull requests, syncing, and merge conflicts.</p>
<p>And if all that doesn’t scare you, then it makes way more sense just to start there anyway. Claude Design is an extra step that, frankly, sits in a really awkward middle spot between Figma and Claude Code.</p>
<p>You might as well stick to Figma.</p>
<p>Of course, most designers on a bigger team would want to use Claude Design and then hand off to engineers, much like you would from Figma.</p>
<p>This is fine, in theory, but no one really saved any time. Your engineers now have to recreate your implementation from scratch, which may divert from the existing design system in subtle ways. You’re back to <a href="https://www.builder.io/blog/designers-can-ship-without-engineering-handoffs">all the existing problems with design handoffs.</a></p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-47 css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-47"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-2g builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-2g"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h2 id="a-better-design-handoff-exists" tabindex="0" role="button" aria-label="Copy link to "A better design handoff exists" section" title="Click to copy link to "A better design handoff exists" section" style="cursor: pointer; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">A better design handoff exists<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-45 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-45"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>We’ve thought about designers, AI, and handoffs and awful lot, and we’ve designed common workflows right into our product.</p>
<p>Claude Design is strong when you want to explore what should exist. <a href="https://builder.io/blog/fusion">Builder</a> is stronger when you need to safely change what already exists.</p>
<p>Builder connects to your team’s repo one time (devs can set this up), and then anyone on your team can access <a href="https://www.builder.io/blog/git-branching-for-designers">their own branches</a> with a live preview, a visual editor, and AI (Claude, GPT, whatever you like) that can see and manipulate your real app inside a safe sandbox.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-2k css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-2k"><div class="css-79elbk"><video autoplay="autoplay" loop="" preload="metadata" class="builder-video css-cz4v3a" muted="muted" playsinline="playsinline"></video><div class="css-1m8i70l"></div></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-46 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-46"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Any changes you make use your real design tokens and real code components. And you can test them out against real data in your application.</p>
<p>When you’re done, you can tag in the proper team for review, and they can continue work on the same branch, making sure that you’re prototyping effort—the tokens, time, and taste you put in—don’t go to waste. No more design fidelity loss.</p>
<p>This also makes it far easier to make small design tweaks that would otherwise be backlogged for eternity. You might be browsing your team’s marketing site and notice a button that needs to move 2px to the left. Great. Just open the editor, move the button, and hit submit.</p>
<p>Your engineers always get final say, so you’ll never break production. You’re free to play around without worrying about jumping between a bunch of different tools. And if you do still want to <a href="https://www.builder.io/blog/turn-a-figma-landing-page-into-a-live-website">roundtrip with Figma</a>, that’s completely supported.</p></span></div></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-49 css-0" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-49"><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-2p builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-2p"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><h3>You can still use Claude Design with Builder</h3></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-48 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-48"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>If you still want to use Claude Design, but you’re interested in Builder, <a href="https://www.builder.io/c/docs/integrate-with-claude-design">we also support importing your Claude Design projects</a> at any phase of your workflow.</p></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-33 builder-has-component css-tna8xt" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-33"><table class="builder-table" style="width: 100%; position: relative; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; outline: none; transition: 0.2s; text-decoration: none;">Claude Design is a hint at the future of design workflows<div class="header-icon-container" aria-hidden="true" style="height: 24px; width: 24px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; visibility: hidden; opacity: 0.7;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(135deg);"><path d="M10 13a5 5 0 0 0 7.54.54l3-3a5 5 0 0 0-7.07-7.07l-1.72 1.71"></path><path d="M14 11a5 5 0 0 0-7.54-.54l-3 3a5 5 0 0 0 7.07 7.07l1.71-1.71"></path></svg></div></h2></span></div><div class="builder-block builder-mok6usgp-4a builder-has-component css-fyr64g" builder-id="builder-mok6usgp-4a"><span class="builder-text css-1qggkls"><p>Claude Design is genuinely innovative. It makes AI feel less like a blank chat box and more like a creative partner: one that can ask questions, generate options, expose sliders, accept annotations, and turn rough intent into something visual.</p>
<p>But the closer you get to production, the more the workflow depends on handoff: to a coding agent, to the repo, and to the team's review process.</p>
<p>Builder takes a different path. Instead of making a better artifact to hand off, it gives designers a visual way to work in the existing app, with the repo, preview, branch, and PR flow already handled.</p>
<p>Claude Design is for exploring what should exist. Builder is for safely changing what already exists.</p></span></div></div><img aria-hidden="true" alt="" role="presentation" width="0" height="0" class="builder-block builder-pixel-3gkia0q3tti css-ke1ct7" builder-id="builder-pixel-3gkia0q3tti" src="https://cdn.builder.io/api/v1/pixel?apiKey=YJIGb4i01jvw0SRdL5Bt"></div></div></div></div><div class="builder-component builder-component-e5669d9ce4db4ce9aff7502675fe30a9" data-name="blog-below-article" data-source="Rendered by Builder.io"><div class="builder-content" builder-content-id="392361c703f546ffb527738e80557359" builder-model="blog-below-article"><div data-builder-component="blog-below-article" data-builder-content-id="e5669d9ce4db4ce9aff7502675fe30a9"><style class="builder-custom-styles">.interactive{
